title: Lecture8
body:
Value at Risk (VaR)   •“What loss level is What does such that we are VaR look X% confident it will not be at? exceeded in N business days?”   Regulators base the Regulators: Want to capital they require banks to keep on VaR know banks’ solvency The market-risk capital is k times the requirements 10-day 99% VaR

Why is VaR where k is at least 3.0

important Exchanges: How solvent are

(and to exchanges (cf. FTX)?

whom)? Portfolios: How much is at risk?   VaR is the loss level that will not be exceeded with a specified probability

C-VaR (or expected shortfall) is the VaR and expected loss given that the loss is greater than the VaR level CVaR • If things get bad, how much will we lose • It is the expected value if the loss exceeds that VaR level

Although C-VaR is theoretically more appealing, it is not widely used  VaR and CVaR  Note on time horizons

Typically ,we calculate VaR on a one-day basis and then annualize (etc) it

For example, Instead of calculating the 10-day, 99% VaR directly analysts usually calculate a 1-day 99% VaR and assume 𝑉𝑎𝑅 10 − 𝑑𝑎𝑦 = 10 × 𝑉𝑎𝑅(1 − 𝑑𝑎𝑦)

This is exactly true when portfolio changes on successive days come from independent identically distributed normal distributions  Example

We have a position worth $10 million in Microsoft shares

The volatility of Microsoft is 2% per day (about 32% per year)

We use N=10 and X=99 (i.e., we want to look at the 1% tale over 10 days)  Example

The 1-day standard deviation in dollar terms is $200,000 (i.e., 2% × $10𝑚)

The standard deviation over 10-days is then $200,000 × 10 = $632,456

Now, we must look at z-scores.

• Here, we have a 1% tail (i.e., z-score is 2.33) • Thus, we have a loss of 2.33 × $632,456 = $1,473,621  What then is the calculation? • To get the 𝛼% 𝑉𝑎𝑅, we use z-scores • The z-score is: 𝑥−𝜇 𝑧𝛼 = 𝜎 • In the prior example, we assume that 𝜇 = 0, which is fine for short time periods • Thus, for one day we have: 𝑥−0 2.33 = 200,000 • Therefore, for 10 days we have 2.33 × 200,000 × 10 = 1,473,621.39  Another example • Calculate the 10 day 99% VAR of a $50 million Index portfolio that has an annual standard deviation of 10.25%. Assume 252 trading days in a year.  Another example • Question: • Calculate the 10 day 99% VAR of a $50 million Index portfolio that has an annual standard deviation of 10.25%. Assume 252 trading days in a year. • Answer: • Here, our z-score is 2.33 0.1025 • The daily standard deviation is 0.1025 • The 10-day standard deviation is then × 10 𝑥 • The 10-day VaR is then 2.33 = 𝜎×$50𝑚 • This gives us 𝑥 = 2,378,753.94  Portfolio example

Suppose we form a portfolio of Microsoft and Telstra • $5 million in Telstra • $10 million in Microsoft Volatilities: • The daily volatility of Telstra is 1% (approx 16% per year) • The volatility of Microsoft is 2% per day (about 32% per year) • Correlation between returns of 0.3  Questions

What is the 1% VaR?

What is the benefit (if any) from diversification as it relates to VaR?  Consider the Telstra position

Consider a position of $5 million in Telstra

The daily volatility of Telstra is 1% (approx 16% per year)

The S.D per 10 days is • Position size x daily s.d. x sqrt(#days) • $50,000 10 = $158,144 The VaR is then 2.33 × $158,144  Consider the portfolio

• The portfolio variance is: 𝜎𝑝2 = 𝜎𝑥2 + 𝜎𝑦2 + 2𝜌𝜎𝑥 𝜎𝑦 • Where, 𝜎𝑥 and 𝜎𝑦 are the standard deviations with the position sizes appropriately incorporated • Here, σX = 200,000 and σY = 50,000 and 𝜌 = 0.3 • Therefore, 𝜎𝑝 = 220,227  Consider the portfolio

We can now obtain the 10-day VaR • 10-day VaR: 𝜎𝑝 × 10 × 𝑧𝛼 = 220,227 × 10 × 2.33 = $1,622,657

The benefits of diversification • Compare VaR of the portfolio vs the VaRs of the two individually • For the portfolio: $1,622,657 • For the individual components: 1,473,621+368,405 • Diversification benefits $219,369   • Calculate the 10 day VAR of an $100 million Index Arbitrage portfolio that is long $50 million in the S&P 500, which has Index arbitrage a annual standard deviation of 10.40%, and short $50 million in the Nasdaq, which example has a annual standard deviation of 14.76%. The correlation between the two indices is .932  Historical simulation / Data

We can also look at historical data for an indication of volatility for VaR

Let’s look at an example…
